chunk_summary: This section outlines clinical markers to assess breastfeeding adequacy in infants under six months, emphasizing weight trajectory, feeding mechanics, and early intervention to prevent complications like hyperbilirubinemia and failure to thrive.
learning_objectives: Identify clinical markers of adequate breastfeeding intake in infants under six months.
Understand weight trajectory expectations, including regaining birth weight by 10–14 days.
Recognize signs of feeding issues and implement stepwise interventions.
Evaluate feeding mechanics and maternal/infant health during supervised feeds.
key_facts: By day 4–5, term infants should have ≥6 wet diapers per day and stools transitioning to yellow.
Term infants typically regain birth weight by 10–14 days after lactogenesis is established.
Persistent sleepiness, brick-dust urate crystals after day 3, or inconsolable hunger are red flags.
Crossing percentiles downward or flattening weight-for-age curves is more concerning than stable low percentiles.
Early intervention includes optimizing latch, increasing feeding frequency, and reassessing weight/hydration within 24–48 hours.
reference_quote: Track weight on the same scale when possible. Some early weight loss is expected, but a term infant should start gaining once lactogenesis is established and should typically regain birth weight by 10–14 days. (American Academy of Pediatrics)
document_key_topics: Signs of feeding issues and when to intervene
